desc:
The fields continue here, full of the same lush green grass as most of the
nearby land. Mostly it seems flat all around, except to the north where the
fields rise up into a sudden bluff. To the south a line of sparse alder trees
cut across the land, their sinuous canopies waving in the wind.
name: A Meadow Bordered by Trees

desc:
These white-trunked trees are located widely apart, thus manoeuvring through the
grove is fairly easy. North of this stand of trees are miles of lush green
meadowland, with a small rise in the distance the only break in the horizon. To
the south, the bases of the trees are immersed in small pools of water.
name: Among a Stand of Alder Trees

desc:
The trees here are sparse, their canopies little more than a bush-like tangle of
sinuous branches and leaves. The ground below is covered in an ankle-high layer
of grass, with the occasional shrub here and there. North of this grove are lush
fields, stretching for miles. The trees to the south are in parts surrounded by
water, their roots dipping into the pools.
name: A Stand of Alder Trees

desc:
This clearing is on a small rise, surrounded by wetlands to the east and a line
of alder trees to the north. There is a small smoke pit built here, with signs
of recent use, and a wooden shanty to the west. Apparently, someone has made a
permanent camp here.
name: A Secluded Clearing

desc:
The ground here is soaked, almost as a swamp in parts. Large pools of water lie
everywhere, with tall reeds sprouting like pointing fingers. Dry land is still
the majority, but even there the water squelches out of every footprint. The
pools do not appear to be deep at least, and should not impede any traveller
other than wetting their ankles. West the land rises and dries out, while east
the pools of water become more abundant.
name: Receding Wetlands

desc:
This tiny shack was built in a hurry, with pieces of wood in different stages of
decay. There are several holes in the wall, where the wood does not line up. The
floor is made of dirt, and there is only a small table with trash piled upon it.
In one corner there is some musty hay, which must be the bed. At least the roof
is sound, since the floor is mostly dry.
name: A Poorly Built Hideaway

desc:
Large pools of water have overtaken the land here. There are hardly any dry
patches left. Tall reeds abound, bordering the pools and restricting your view.
The water is more like a shallow lake than dry land in parts, complete with the
splashing of fish and the croaking of frogs.
name: Saturated Marshland

desc:
Dry land is scarce here, although even there the earth is muddy and saturated
with water. Tall, green reeds of grass with brown tips grow in large clumps
along the edges of the shallow water, limiting your view. The water itself is
stagnant, unmoving except in the wake of some small animal swimming.
name: Flooded Marshland

desc:
Here the earth is largely below water, with only the tops of the grass and
cattails sticking up out of a constant foot of water. The tips of the grass seem
to make little islands in the water, but it is an illusion - there is no dry
land here. North some giant thick brush mingles with the reeds to form an
impenetrably tangled barrier, while east the water runs into a small pond in the
plains.
name: Watery Fields

desc:
The ground here is covered in shallow pools of stagnant, murky brown water. The
muddy ground in-between sinks slightly, trying to swallow those who treads upon
it. Clumps of reeds appear here and there, creating secluded nesting places for
wildlife. North there is an abandoned way-station atop a small hill, which once
housed a guard post and an inn for travellers on the Great South Road. To the
south water trickles out of these wetlands and on through the plains in a small
stream.
name: A Shallow Pond

desc:
Little more than a streamlet dribbling through the dustlands, this tiny line of
water is one of many tributaries to the Isen, which it feeds into far south of
here. It draws its waters from the wetlands around the Old South Road and,
skirting around the edges of the hill, carries on through the plains. The water
looks unclear, a little coloured by the soil maybe, but perfectly drinkable.
name: Small Stream amongst the Flatlands

desc:
Born from the wetlands a few miles north of here, this little stream is one of
many tributaries to the mighty Isen a long way distant. Somehow it trickles and
dribbles its way that far through the seemingly flat plains, pushed onwards by
the constant flow of rainwater. It has some practical uses too, as a source of
water for the Dunlending camp atop the hill just north of here. Every morning
large amounts of water are collected onto a wheeled contraption, then hauled up
the extremely steep southwestern hillside with a long rope and many horses.
name: A Tiny Brook

desc:
Miles of gently undulating plains are the norm in southern Enedwaith, and here
is no exception. The nearest break in that pattern is a low flat hill several
miles northwards. Even more distantly lie the White Mountains, on the horizon to
the southeast, so named for their snow-capped peaks. Even at this distance their
size is obvious; up close they must be truly towering. Just east of here a small
trail picks its way through the terrain without trouble, one of the multitude of
Dunlending paths that criss-cross these lands.
name: Endless Prairie

desc:
These long plains are part of the huge upland of Enedwaith, which stretches
between the great rivers of Greyflood on the borders of Eregion, and the Isen,
still a long way south of here. Mostly they are uninhabited, with an occasional
smattering of isolated Dunlending towns here and there. One of those towns can
be seen a few miles north of here, atop a low flat hill. A path runs south from
there, passing by just east of you, and continuing south until it passes out of
your eyesight.
name: Flat Dunland Plains

desc:
The land is flat all around, for almost as far as the eye can see. The only
exception is the faint impression left on the southern horizon by the White
Mountains. Just east of here a small path runs north to south, but it too is
subsumed into the plains, disappearing over both horizons. Quite possibly it is
as much of a landmark as a path, as it would seem to be rather easy to get lost
amongst the immensity of flat land, without any other landmark as a guide.
name: Uninhabited Plains

desc:
Like a dotted line, infrequently marked by little white stones, this path makes
its way across the flatlands. It appears to have no obvious end either to the
north or south, stretching to both horizons and beyond. Underfoot it is merely
well-trodden earth, with the ruts of wheelmarks and hooves visible. Far away to
the southeast lie the White Mountains, the only feature on the landscape. All
else is merely the plains of Enedwaith, stretching out endlessly.
name: On a Small Trail

desc:
Like a smooth lawn the plains stretch out here, the grass little more than a
tiny coverlet underfoot. The occasional sign of wildlife can be seen, with the
burrows of small creatures and the droppings of larger ones. Eastwards the land
becomes slightly less smooth as it approaches the downs, while west a path runs
south to north. South the plains continue as far as you can see, without end it
would seem.
name: Grassy Flatlands

desc:
Not much of a feature these downs, but in comparison with the flat plains they
do stick out. Covered with knee-high bushes, and a few clumps of trees, they are
home to most of the wildlife in this part of the lands . It is probably not
surprising to find that the locals hunt here, and indeed the Dunlendings of the
nearby towns can be found here most months of the year. Some hunting paths wind
over and around the downs, obviously frequently used.
name: Undulating Grasslands

desc:
This paddock is enclosed by a small wooden fence, built to stop the animals from
wandering. The ground is covered with short grass, recently grazed. There are
several fields within the fence, some closed off so that the vegetation can
regrow. Some troughs line the barn wall, filled with water. Outside the fenced
area are fields that are no longer planted.
name: An Animal Paddock

desc:
Close to the north there is a wooden fence, and beyond it a small farm. Here the
ground is covered with hay and small flower beds. Sometimes the hay moves
rapidly as if there were something big moving amongst the grain, but it is an
illusion of the wind. In the sky, several birds can be seen surveying the land
below for prey, as it is abundant with wildlife. Directly south of here runs an
old road, a barely surviving line of stones.
name: South of a Fence

desc:
The road here was made with great attention to detail. One can see that the
builders placed each of the flagstones next to each other with precision, even
though time has taken its toll. Their workmanship can still be seen though, in
the straight line of its route, and the little dikes that drain water. The land
to the north of this great road is mainly gentle rolling plains before the main
mountain range far to the north.
name: The Great South Road

desc:
Once this road ran all the way from Osgiliath in the south, to Annuminas in the
north, passing through countless lands. All along its length it was paved, and
constructed level and true for swift travel. This wonder of engineering was
built by the Numenoreans to link the two kingdoms, and saw much traffic in its
heyday. Since the destruction of Arnor near on a millennium ago, its use has
dropped to nothing, and unmaintained it has fallen into disrepair. Nowadays most
travellers will spurn the crumbled causeways upon which the road mounts to the
northwest, preferring to skirt around the edges of the wetlands.
name: The Great South Road

desc:
Like the land of Rohan to the east, the plains of Enedwaith have little more
than gentle undulations. This particular area is slightly wetter than the norm
though, with frequent pools of stagnant rainwater to be seen all around. Just
north of here the Old South Road was built half an age ago, raised up slightly
on a causeway to protect it on the rainier days. The only other noticeable
landmark nearer than the distant mountains is a Dunlending town atop a long flat
hill, several miles to the northwest.
name: Flat Stretch of Plains

desc:
A faint shift in the terrain is apparent here, almost like a borderland. South
the plains roll by in irregular waves, whereas north the grasslands are as flat
as a still pond. Threading through the heather are infrequent hunting paths,
often faint and easy to lose. Mostly the only one through is to trample over the
soft two foot heather. Although easy on the feet, it's a wearying way to travel.
name: Heather-Clad Downs

desc:
Like ocean waves the grassy downs roll over the land, covered in parts with
great swathes of soft heather. Here and there the small hunting trails of the
Dunlendings can be seen, used as frequently by those on four legs as those on
two. Occasionally you come across a small camp, well hidden by the undulating
landscape - obviously well used, someone must live off this land. Far to the
east the downs give way to grey barren plains, where broken stone protrudes
through the earth.
name: Rolling Downs

desc:
This is just another small camp, like many the Dunlendings have built in these
lands. The hunting is excellent around here, with small rabbits, voles and foxes
in the heathery downs east of here, and much larger game - deer and some elks -
to be found in the open plains. Around the campfire here the Dunlendings can be
found most nights, boasting of their exploits and slugging down their coarse
beer.
name: A Dunlending Hunting Camp

desc:
Straight north to south across the wide plain goes this path, from the large
Dunlending town of Wulfston to the crossing points on the Isen far south. Here
it passes by a small camp on the eastern side, where the Dunlendings stop by on
their long hunting trips. The path itself is little more than a track worn by
feet and hooves, marked occasionally by white stones but completely unpaved.
name: Trail across the Plains

desc:
Through these flat plains a path is not truly necessary, and indeed this one is
just used to mark the swiftest route between two points. Northwards it leads to
a Dunlending town, and many miles south to a boating point on the swift river
Isen. It is unpaved, although with a white stone every hundred yards marking its
side. Just north of here a small stream can be seen, bending away to the west.
name: Small Dunlending Trail

desc:
Spotted here and there with streaks of whins, these plains are nevertheless
fairly easy to cross. Small hunting paths criss-cross the land, occasionally
passing through recently used camps. The Dunlendings who frequent them live most
of the year on these plains, hunting rabbits and boar. Some distance to the west
a larger path can be made out, travelling north-south. It disappears over both
horizons, and out of your sight.
name: Open Plains

desc:
Firmer underfoot than some of the wetlands north of here, these plains are
rather featureless. The occasional thicket of trees and wiry bushes breaks the
skyline in parts, but generally there is little in the way of cover. Not far
west of here a path can be seen going south towards the horizon, and north to
the base of a Dunlending hill-town. A commanding position for a fort or town, it
must be the home of a mighty overlord of these lands.
name: Grassy Flatlands

desc:
Worn into the plains rather than built, this path takes a route straight north
and south from here. Little in the terrain obstructs it, other than infrequent
hollows. It serves as a supply and trading route for the large Dunlending fort
north of here, sited on the crown of a low flat hillock. The trail runs to the
eastern base of that hill, and then splits. One fork winds up the hillside to
the only gates, while the other continues north to the remains of the Great
South Road.
name: Trail through the Plains

desc:
Two rough trails meet here, at the eastern end of a long low hill. One heads a
short length northwards to the Old South Road, and straight south through the
plains. The other slowly ascends the hillside to the west, winding to lessen the
climb. It is the only road up to the Dunlending fortress-camp, surrounded by
earthen embankments along the crown of the hill. From here you can just pick out
where it enters the town, passing through gates in the wooden ring.
name: Trail below a Hill

desc:
The lands of the Gap are wide, and differ greatly from place to place. Here the
earth is usually somewhat wet, full of small pools and damp grass, as the light
topsoil fails to absorb much of the rains that sweep across the flat lands of
Enedwaith. Realising this, the engineers of Numenor built their great road on a
causeway, the remains of which still survive to the northeast. West of here a
small hill overlooks the surrounding plains, with a well defended town atop it.
name: Plains by the Roadside

desc:
Light moss covers the remaining paving stones here, although most have long
since crumbled into gravel. This was once a great road, linking Arnor and Gondor
over countless hundreds of miles. Now it is utterly decrepit, nothing more than
a line of stones through the grasslands.
name: The Great South Road

desc:
The road has succumbed to the erosion of the water to the west, and now is only
a muddy mix of gravel and small pools. The few remains of the once giant paving
stones are covered in mud and slimy algae. Some stones have become so dislodged
that their edges stick up out of the mud to trip up any unwary traveller. The
road continues up away from the wetlands to the north, and travels through the
plains to the east.
name: Muddy Remains of an Old Road

desc:
The causeway which carries this road for many miles to the north-west begins
here, near a deserted hilltop settlement to the west. East the lands are gentle
rolling plains of well-kept farmlands, while west beyond the hills are mostly
flat wetlands. These rains have made the soil in this land very fertile, but in
parts the land floods when the water cannot drain away. Southwards the old road
crumbles in a sunken muddy mess.
name: The Great South Road

desc:
The worn remains of the Great South Road run around the base of a hillock here,
where the ruins of a small settlement can be seen. The buildings are destitute
and no people can be seen living there anymore. The road continues west on its
causeway through the wetlands in a dreadful condition, pockmarked with holes and
jagged slabs. South it lowers to the level of the plains.
name: The Tattered Remains of a Road

desc:
Probably not long ago abandoned, these fields now grow wild wheat in places and
plain hay in others. Here and there the ground is so wet that nothing can grow,
probably why they were abandoned. Just east of here there is a small wooden
fence, enclosing some grazing areas. North of it are the farm buildings.
name: Forgotten Fields

desc:
The grass is fairly short here, and in places there are piles of rocks and
stones on the ground. There could have been buildings here some time long ago,
but now nothing remains of them. From the number and locations of these piles
one can count two, maybe three separate buildings. Over the road to the west
there are a couple of buildings still standing atop a small hillside.
name: Remains of Some Buildings

desc:
This courtyard was once a fairly busy place, bustling with travellers and the
guards posted here to watch the road. Since the road is now all but forgotten,
the way station has long been abandoned. All that remains now is a small and
overgrown gravel path leading to the centre of the courtyard, and two of the
stone buildings. West was the stable yard, while north was the old inn. Nothing
remains of the barracks to the north-west except scattered piles of stones. The
courtyard slopes away downward to the south to a small muddy pond, and eastward
down the hillside onto the great road.
name: An Overgrown Courtyard

desc:
Here, pools of murky green water gather, reflecting the grass underneath. Along
the edges of these pools of water grow tall reeds of grass and cattails. These
tufts of vegetation make excellent nesting grounds, making the plains almost
like the side of a lake. South, the reeds grow into thick brush, and become
utterly impassable. East is the western side of a hillock, a steep bluff with no
route up.
name: Saturated Marshland

desc:
There are few patches of dry area in this land, almost completely flooded with
water. Even the ground that appears dry seeps with water if any pressure is
applied to it. The vegetation is sparse and tough, mostly near-dead brown grass
and rustling ferns. Much small wildlife can be heard rustling about, but always
out of sight. The wetlands continue in all directions, although receding to the
north-west.
name: Soggy Marshland

desc:
The large pools of water are not so predominant here as in the terrain to the
east and south. There are tall reeds bordering the pools, excellent nesting
places for several species of bird. Still, there is enough dry ground to follow
a trail of sorts through here. To the south and east the pools grow in size,
number and depth, and there is far less dry land around.
name: Receding Wetlands

desc:
These trees border the wetlands to the south and east, buffering them from the
encroaching water. The trees are spread sparsely, which allows plenty of light
during the day through their canopy of sinuous branches and leaves. Underfoot
the ground is covered with light short grass and occasional puddles of water,
submersing the roots of some of the trees.
name: A Strip of Alder Trees

desc:
Here the land is flat and is covered with rich, green grass that is knee deep in
spots. There is evidence of grazing in some areas where the grass has been
chewed down to the ground. In the south, there is a line of alder trees. The
trees continues to the east, while to the north the fields begin to gently rise
up on the side of a bluff.
name: A Lush Meadow

desc:
Small to mid-size pools of water pepper the ground here. Being a slight bit
higher than the surrounding land it is not as wet as south or north of here,
where the land is completely saturated. Looking around it seems as if these
wetlands are stagnant, the water unable to drain away. What little vegetation
that grows here is brown and barely clinging to life, unable to draw much life
from the poor soil.
name: Raised Wetlands

desc:
The ground in this area is too saturated to take in any more water. If even the
gentlest pressure is applied the water beneath the grass seeps up and tries to
cover the object displacing it. To the north and east an old earthen causeway
carries a road through the wetlands. Its sides are slippery, the many small
stones eroded from its banks making footing uncertain.
name: Grassland Soaked with Water

desc:
This road is near impassable, pitted with eroded marks. In parts the old paving
stones give way to tip up the unwary traveller. What used to be the Great South
Road turns slightly eastward here to pass around a hillock. The Numenoreans
built a way-station there long ago, to provide food and shelter for travellers
between Arnor and Gondor. The buildings are ruined now, with only the walls
remaining. The road continues north-west, where it rises on an causeway to cut
through the wetlands, and south-east towards the Gap of Rohan.
name: The Tattered Remains of a Road

desc:
Twenty feet above the already elevated hilltop town, there is an excellent view
of the surrounding plains to be had here. The tower consists of a flat wooden
top, with waist-high handrails on all sides but the west, where a rope ladder
dangles down to the earthworks below. The tower itself appears in very poor
condition, and creaks alarmingly most of the time. In the frequent high winds
that streak across the Dunland plains it must seem positively perilous to be on
watch duty.
name: An Unstable Watchtower

desc:
Between a large barn-like structure and the natural line of the earth walls, the
track bends westward here. This is the dingiest part of the village, with litter
everywhere and frequent street robbery. Small tents, mostly homes, lie on both
sides of the track. The Dunlendings have never shaken off their hillman past,
even though these ones have long since moved from those lands. Some way to the
south lies the main gates, while to the west a few small buildings are scattered
about, mainly shops.
name: Small Trail by the Fortress Walls

desc:
Moving between the huge number of tents, barns and small buildings, the track
here follows the side of a long flat building. Most of it is taken up by the pub
to the west, with a small tailor's shop built onto its eastern end. Through the
open doors can be seen countless furs and leather clothing, of all shapes and
sizes. The track continues to the west, bending around yet another large tent.
North are the imposing earthen walls, topped with sharpened stakes.
name: Path along the Earthworks

desc:
More a muddy trail than anything else, it is dank and gloomy here, overhung by
the fortifications to the north. Little sunlight gets at the ground, and it has
become filthy with litter, creating a stinking corner of the village where none
but the poorest choose to live. Periodically the Dunlendings lay down wooden
planks to traverse the muddy pools, but most of the year the only way through is
to walk knee deep into them. The cheapest pub around lies to the south,
partially sinking into the permanently wet earth.
name: Back Alleys

desc:
Wulfston is known for its countless tents, and nowhere is this more apparent
than here. Tents lie to both sides of the track as far as the eye can see, the
homes of the common townsfolk. Not far to the north are the walls, earthworks of
some height with tall wooden stakes topping them. A walkway runs along their
crest, but there is no way up from here. Turning along the base of the walls,
the track enters a muddy and smelly part of the village. A seedy pub lies off
the track along there, a wood and canvas building of dubious construction.
name: Track through the Tents

desc:
Covered in small stones, this is one of the gathering areas for celebrations and
the like. It is surrounded to the west by the earthworks that are the camp's
defences, and to the east by the fighing pits. North leads to a dingy trail, and
south a large open air tent. Mostly this place is busy round the clock with
markets, fighting, story-telling or sometimes even just as a storage area.
name: Open Square

desc:
A huge tent, made out of animal hides stitched together, protects the square
from the elements. It is fixed at each corner by a stone block, and hung from a
thick tree trunk in the centre. Around this central support is the seat of the
chieftain, surrounded by countless thick animal furs and some well-crafted
wooden chairs. The vast sagging tent overhead covers a large enough area for
many sporting events, when the weather does not allow them to be held outside.
name: A Large Open-Air Tent

desc:
This wooden platform slightly overhangs the steep hillside having been built on
countless ramshackle wooden branches. The platform is filled with coils of rope
that are attached to a few large barrels. Most of the fort's water is supplied
this way, hauled up from the stream below by overworked oxen. Northwards lies
the town square, covered over by a large open air tent.
name: A Wooden Platform

desc:
These metalworks are contained in a spacious room, dominated by a primitive
furnace on its southern wall. Much wood is stacked near to it, along with the
other tools of the smith - bellows, tongs, hammers, and many shapes of moulds
and anvils. The secret of metalworking is rather new to the Dunlendings, and the
resulting products are not of the highest quality. Many broken or failed
attempts at weapons and hauberks are strewn about the earthen floor, discarded
in disgust by the novice smiths. However a few exceptions hanging on wall-pegs
draw the eye. Broken in battle, but still showing signs of fine workmanship,
most of the shields and chain-mail bear the designs of the horse-lords.
name: The Metalworks

desc:
There are only half a dozen stalls here, made of roughly cut wooden logs and
covered over by a hanging cow-hide roof. The Dunlendings, being by nature a
hillman people, have never really adapted to the horseback form of fighting,
unlike their Rohirrim enemies. Still, on occasion visitors come by horseback,
usually the few outside merchants trusted enough to trade with. They may be
allowed to keep their mounts here, but the care taken of them is questionable to
say the least. Judging by the filthy stalls and the uncaring attitude of the
stable-man, it might be foolhardy to stable any horse here.
name: Stables

desc:
Most of the south-east corner of the town is a large field of trampled earth,
with the occasional patch of grass. In the wild ungoverned lands of Enedwaith
there are many threats, from rival Dunlendings to Rohirrim, and even marauding
orcs from the north. These grounds are used to hone their skills against such
enemies, and for some basic military manoeuvres. West of here are the stables,
little more than a few tented stalls, and east the barracks. South are the high
earthen defences, with no way up onto the ramparts from here.
name: Training Ground

desc:
Lines of beds fill this long flat tent, one of the biggest in the fort. It is
home to the village guards, mostly mercenaries brought in from afar who will not
challenge the village's leaders. Most of the barracks is untidy and fairly
dirty, with clothes and possessions strewn all around. Dunlendings do not cope
well with living inside, used as they are to the nomadic hillman life.
name: Rough Barracks

desc:
Passing through a gap in the steeply piled earth surrounding the village, the
area between the two gates is the first line of defence for its inhabitants. In
here any attacking force would be caught in a hail of spears and arrows cast
down from the wooden ramparts, assuming they were not first overpowered by the
gate-guards. The main outside gates, each a dozen huge wooden poles tied with
ropes of vines, are usually only closed at night, while the inside ones are only
ever used in a siege. On the south side is a little covered area, where the
guards try to escape the bitter eastern winds that sweep across from Rohan.
name: Between the Gates

desc:
You are underneath a watchtower, built on four thick beams above the camp's
defensive earthworks. The wood itself looks heavily weather-beaten and is in
terrible shape, rotten and flaking away in parts. Those unlucky enough to be
chosen for watch duty are often exposed to driving wind and rain on the exposed
tower, and can only hope the creaking platform doesn't collapse below them. The
only way up is via a twenty foot rope ladder, swinging in the wind above you.
name: Below the Watchtower

desc:
Most of the north of the camp is a sprawling expanse of wooden buildings and
tented lodgings, built wherever there is a spare plot of land. Just west of here
is a small inn, a rough building with a thatched roof. East leads up the
earthworks to a wooden watchtower overlooking the surrounding flatlands, a high
and often creaking platform. The beams look more than a little unstable, due to
the bad workmanship and wet rot.
name: Winding Track through the Canvas-Roofed Buildings

desc:
This shop consists of one long tented room, with the tailor's wares hung on pegs
on the supports. Looking closer all types of clothing can be seen - from leather
trousers to long capes, from sandals to fur-lined boots. The old man who owns
the shop is a prolific clothes-maker, and must take in quite a lot of money from
the Dunlendings. He has no fears of robbery or assault also, as he is under the
protection of Wulfston's fearsome leader, who values good clothes.
name: Tailor's Shop

desc:
Large blocks of uneven stone were laid here near the monument, many centuries
ago. Mostly the paving stones have cracked or worn, and many now lie broken and
splintered, leaving frequent hazardous potholes. To the west the paving gives
way to a large square covered by a cavernous open-air tent. North, at the very
centre of the village, are the fighting pits, where captured beasts and men are
thrown to fight for the amusement of the Dunlendings.
name: Remnants of Paving

desc:
Standing somewhat incongruously amongst the countless tents are many ancient
pillars, originally arranged in a large arc. Most have fallen into ruin, but the
four largest on the western side still stand undiminished. They are built of a
dull black rock that weather and time leave no mark on. Running in-between these
monuments from another age is a trail, leading west towards an enormous open-air
tent, and east to the gates.
name: Trail through an Ancient Monument

desc:
Most of the day these streets are bustling with merchants and traders, and the
wagons carrying their goods. This is the only Dunlending settlement to accept
contact, even stretching to outside trade, because of its leader's liking for
foreign-made luxuries. The main path heads west from here, while a smaller side
street full of dingy shops and tented homes leads north. Just to the east are
the gates - sturdy constructions made of many long wooden poles.
name: Trail before the Gates

desc:
You are by the gates of a Dunlending camp, which lies atop a low flat hill in
the plains. This is the fortress of Wulfston, named after the Dunlending chief
who ransacked Rohan nearly a century ago. It has survived to this day; besieged
occasionally, but never captured due to the natural defences. The main gate is
just to the west of you, through a gap in the earthworks. That fortification
wall circles the crown of the hill, topped by sharp ten foot wooden stakes. It
is clear to see that this fortress would take some power to storm.
name: Before the Gates

desc:
So named after some obscure and long forgotten Dunlending legend, this place is
a fairly cosy pub. Built of red clay and roofed with thatch over low wooden
beams, it is probably the most popular drinking spot in the village. The choice
of drink is limited though, with only a few different barrels available behind
the bar. A small hallway leads out to a few bedrooms, where guests can stay for
the night. The place is rather less than clean though, and anyone staying in
those rooms might find they wake up with fleas or lice.
name: The Toad and Whistle Inn

desc:
This large tent is one of the few shops in the settlement, selling anything from
basic outdoors items like rope and lanterns, to food. Some of this he bakes
himself, but mostly he just buys meat straight from the hunters who live nearby.
His produce is mostly lain out on dirty tables all around the tent, with the
salted cuts of meat hanging from hooks on the tent poles. The only way out is
the door flap on the southern side of the tent.
name: Grocer's Tent

desc:
Few buildings of the Dunlendings are kept clean, but the interior of this pub is
utterly filthy. Obviously no attempt at cleaning has been made in a long time.
The pub itself consists of a central room with plain tables and benches for the
common folk, and private rooms for those held in higher esteem. This place is in
the worst part of the village, frequented by the small underworld population.
Many of the drunken patrons have their purses stolen by some nimble fingered
pickpocket every night, with the bar staff turning a blind eye.
name: Small Pub

desc:
A circular depression creates a natural arena here, allowing many bloodthirsty
Dunlendings to view the pits at one time. Anyone captured in battle, sometimes
even travellers passing by on the old South Road, might find themselves thrown
into the muddy enclosure to fight fierce creatures. Usually these are nothing
more than a large boar or bull, but on days of celebration larger creatures may
be found for the crowd's pleasure. On some occasions even hill trolls and orcs
have been captured, and made to fight until death.
name: The Fighting Pits

desc:
A rickety ladder lies off in the northwest corner, leading to a trapdoor in the
ceiling. A strange writing of some kind, done in white chalk, crawls along the
northwest wall. The writing continues off into the dark to the east and south.
The floor, the ceiling, and the walls are covered with irregular shapes of
limestone that have been carved out naturally, by water, over a long period of
time. There is a smell of mustiness about this cavern.
name: Cavern

desc:
Cavernous walls loom over to produce a tall vaulted ceiling. The cavern is large
here, but devoid of anything. There is no water here, but the walls are moist to
the touch. Along the walls, a flowing script has been written in white chalk,
but there is little meaning to it. The cavern continues to the north and south.
There is some light to the east.
name: Cavern

desc:
This corner of the cavern is covered by the strange script seen from the north
and eastern rooms. Further passage to the west and south are blocked by heavy
boulders and natural limestone formations. At one time, there may have been a
passageway to the west, and this no longer exists. The cavern widens to the east
and north.
name: Cavern

desc:
This is a smaller part of the cavern. The strange writings cover the wall and
continue to the east and west. The are small limestone formations here as well,
which are difficult to maneuver around. A dim light can be seen flickering off
to the north.
name: Cavern

desc:
This room is filled with broken pieces of limestone and many smaller boulders.
The pillar to the north is somewhat cracked in this section, and small rivulets
of water can be seen seeping out. There is no sign of the continuous writing
that can be found in other rooms.
name: Cavern

desc:
This area of the cavern splits in three different directions. Strange odours
arise from the eastern area and there are no lights in any other direction. The
cavern walls are covered in scrawlings and weird writings.
name: Cavern

desc:
Refuse lies in piles everywhere here. There is a strong smell of offal and dung
as well as decaying flesh. Scorch marks can be seen along the walls. Strange
coloured liquids collect in puddles here. Some liquids are luminescent while
others bubble and hiss as though heated by a flame. There is a corpse tucked off
in the northeast corner of this room. It looks as though it has been here a long
time, and there is little outward sign of decay.
name: Botched Experiments

desc:
This cavern is a little more than a tunnel. A great pillar rises to the cavern
wall off to the west. Along the eastern walls, the flowing script written in
chalk continues. The passageway leads to the north and south.
name: Cavern

desc:
This part of the cavern is a little more than a tunnel with small stalagmites
and stalactites forming around a passage. Along the walls the hand, which has
written in white chalk, continues to form indecipherable formulations. A little
to the east there is a dim light. The dark passageways continue to the south and
west.
name: Cavern

desc:
This room smells strongly of filth and refuse. It seems to be used as a room to
sleep in, judging from the pallet and small fire. There is a small pool of water
here to drink from, and it makes this part of the cavern somewhat homely, if not
for the rank smell. The walls are smooth and have scrawlings of some sort of
white chalk.
name: Sleeping Room

desc:
The cavern continues to the east and west from here. There are numerous large
formations of limestone in this area. To the south, one large pillar can be
seen, which is large enough to form an entire wall to this room. The familiar
hand continued to write in foreign script using white chalk along the northern
wall.
name: Cavern

desc:
A large wall to the north is naturally formed with many stalagmites. The ceiling
here is smooth. Along each wall many glyphs and strange writings have been
written with some kind of white chalk. Even the limestone formations have this
writing on them. There is a light to the south, and darkness continues off to
the east and west.
name: Cavern

desc:
Bowls and glassware are stacked everywhere here. Within each container is a
different coloured liquid or powder. The cavern walls lack any writing, which is
so prevalent in the other parts of these caves. This room is very cramped, and
seems at one time to have been a much larger room. There are large piles of rock
where a recent cave-in is evident.
name: Potion Storage

desc:
This room is similar with respect to its surrounding cavern rooms. The ceiling
extends down with stalactites forming pillars around the north, south and
western exits, making them look like actual exits. The cavern here is somewhat
different though, in that it misses the script along the walls like the other
rooms have. The eastern wall is part of a massive pillar that has been formed
naturally with the rest of the cavern. Centered in this room is a large stone
table. There are several torches embedded in the walls in this cavern, which
illuminate this room. The floor and walls have numerous scorch marks and broken
glass is littered everywhere.
name: Underground Laboratory

desc:
This room, secreted away by a convienent cave-in, has many different items of
the surrounding areas. Surprising items from Dunland, Eregion and even a flag
from the city of Tharbad can be found here. Most of the items seen here are
useless, except for the large chest in the southern part of the room.
name: Storage Room
